title: Atom--photon Entanglement with a Single Trapped Cesium Atom
authors: H. Hwang, J. Moon, F. Herzallah, E. Oh, A. Safari, M. Saffman
abstract: We demonstrate atom--photon entanglement using a single cesium atom trapped in an optical tweezer. Entanglement is generated by resonant excitation and subsequent spontaneous decay, which entangles the atomic Zeeman state with photon polarization. The photon is collected with a high numerical aperture objective (NA = 0.55) and coupled into a single-mode fiber, enabling atom photon measurements and measurement of the Bell-state fidelity. We obtain raw entanglement fidelity of ${\mathcal F} = 0.942(16)$ and inferred fidelity of ${\mathcal F}_{\rm inf} = 0.962(26)$ after correcting independently characterized atom measurement errors. Compared with related free-space experiments using $^{87}$Rb, the multilevel structure of the relevant excited state in $^{133}$Cs requires the use of a single short excitation pulse in each entanglement attempt in order to suppress unwanted re-excitation. These results establish a free-space Cs atom--photon interface and provide a step toward dual-species Rb--Cs quantum networking.
summary: The researchers successfully generated and quantified entanglement between a single trapped Cesium atom and an emitted photon. They achieved a high entanglement fidelity ($>94\%$) by carefully controlling the excitation pulse and performing rigorous error analysis. This work establishes Cs as a viable platform for building quantum networks, particularly for dual-species quantum links.
main_problem: The primary goal is to demonstrate and quantify the entanglement fidelity of an atom-photon state generated using a single trapped Cesium atom.
main_result: The authors achieved a raw entanglement fidelity of $F = 0.942(16)$ and an inferred fidelity of $F_{	ext{inf}} = 0.962(26)$, establishing a free-space Cs atom-photon interface.
method: Entanglement is generated via resonant excitation followed by spontaneous decay, and fidelity is measured using Bell-state tomography based on parity oscillations.
model_or_system: The system uses a single Cesium atom trapped in an optical tweezer, where the atomic qubit is encoded in hyperfine states and the photonic qubit in polarization. The process relies on controlled resonant excitation and subsequent decay.
key_observables: Entanglement Fidelity ($F$, $F_{	ext{inf}}$), single-photon correlation function ($g^{(2)}(0)$), coherence times ($T_2^*$), and parity oscillation contrast.
important_parameters: Raw fidelity $F = 0.942(16)$; Inferred fidelity $F_{	ext{inf}} = 0.962(26)$; Single-photon emission $g^{(2)}(0) = 0.096(66)$.
main_assumptions: The inferred fidelity calculation corrects only for independently characterized atomic state detection errors, while other sources like dephasing are treated as physical losses.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the optical setup, the experimental pulse sequence, Rabi oscillations demonstrating coherence times for different qubit states, and measured atom-photon parity oscillations in various bases.
paper_structure: The paper details the experimental setup, measures coherence times and single-photon emission, quantifies entanglement fidelity using Pauli correlations, and finally performs a detailed error budget analysis to extract the highest possible fidelity estimate.
why_interesting: This work provides a high-fidelity, free-space quantum interface using Cs, which is crucial for scaling up quantum networks and connecting different quantum memory nodes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Optical setup and experimental sequence. (a) Experimental setup used for 133Cs atom trapping, single-photon characterization, and atom–photon entanglement verification. For the g(2) measurement, a 50:50 non-polarizing beamsplitter is used, yielding g(2)(0) = 0.096(66). For atom–photon entanglement, the beamsplitter is replaced by a polarizing beamsplitter (PBS), enabling polarization-basis analysis with single-photon counting modules (SPCMs). (b) Cesium atom–photon decay channels and histogram of photon arrival times. (c) Experimental sequence for entanglement generation and verification. In stage (i), a single atom is loaded using a MOT and polarization-gradient cooling. After...

FIG. 2. Coherent control and coherence of the ground-state hyperfine manifold of a trapped 133Cs atom. (a) Left: level diagram illustrating π-polarized microwave control at a bias field B = 1.4 G for the transitions between clock states (black) and |↑⟩↔|↑′⟩ (blue). Center: measured microwave Rabi oscillations as a function of pulse length. Measurement shows Rabi frequency of Ωclock = 2π × 109.0(9) kHz (black) and Ω|↑⟩↔|↑′⟩= 2π × 104.04(59) kHz (blue) Right: Ramsey fringes versus free-evolution time; fits to a decaying sinusoid yield T ∗ 2 = 10.8(7) ms (black) and T ∗ 2 = 130(10) µs (blue). (b) Two-photon stimulated Raman control between |↓⟩and |↑′⟩with T ∗ 2 = 14(1) ms using σ+–σ+ microwave...

FIG. 3. (a), (c), and (e) show the atom–photon parity oscil- lations measured in the Z, X, and Y bases, respectively, as a function of the photon HWP angle θ. Each data point is the average of 100 measurements, with error bars indicating ± one standard deviation. The even-parity outcomes correspond to |↑′⟩|H⟩+ |↓⟩|V ⟩, while the odd-parity outcomes correspond to |↑′⟩|V ⟩+ |↓⟩|H⟩. Solid curves are independent sinusoidal fits to the measured probabilities. (b), (d), and (f) show the reconstructed joint probabilities evaluated at the HWP an- gles that maximize the parity-oscillation visibility in the Z, X, and Y bases, respectively.
